answer:
**The passage is from the *Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana* (unknown author), Chapter 1.**[^1]

In this Old Javanese Buddhist didactic poem, the Buddha (appearing as Vairocana and also identified with Śākyamuni) instructs the pupil(s) Kuñjarakarṇa (an ascetic *rākṣasa*) and Pūrṇavijaya (a *gandharva*/*vidyādhara* king) on the origins of the body during a sermon on karma, rebirth, and the soul. The Buddha explains the seed's emission and embryonic formation precisely as described.[^2]

The relevant verse (Canto 17.8) reads:

> It was from the passion of your father and mother that they were moved to unite.  
> From the *liṅga* of your father you emerged, like molten tin, poured out.  
> Indeed, Kāma is its name; Ratih is your name when you clung to your mother.  
> There in the blossoming lotus of your mother's womb, you took on a definite form.[^3]

This is immediately followed by the embryo's association with the five great elements (earth, water, fire, wind, ether), each linked to one of the five souls (*ātmā*, *parātmā*, *antarātmā*, *nirātmā*, *cetanātmā*), forming the body (Cantos 18–19). The scheme is first introduced earlier by Yama (Canto 11.17), but the Buddha elaborates it here in the context of bodily origin.[^4]

[^1]: *Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana*, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/).

[^2]: *Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana*, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/#p-1082d7).

[^3]: *Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana*, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/#p-1082d7).

[^4]: *Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana*, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/#p-4edd93).
